name: long-agentic-session-stress
description: Compressed proxy for a real long session: 4 plain user prompts in one session. Spec asserts per-turn ready→working→ready arcs with no flicker WITHIN a turn, no stuck state. Tool calls were originally part of the recipe but the modern claudecode default (Sonnet 4.6 + high effort) refuses auto-mode for paid models, which stalls tool-call turns at the permission gate. Plain prompts keep the recording deterministic; the no-flicker-within-turn invariant is still meaningful because each turn still has multiple intermediate assistant chunks even without tool_use.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

long-agentic-session-stress is a compressed proxy for a real long session: several plain user prompts in one live session, several eliciting tool calls, stress-testing that irrlicht's transcript parser stays coherent across a large volume of interleaved assistant fragments and tool-call/result pairs. The spec asserts (a) state stays continuously `working` WITHIN a turn — no flicker — (b) clean `working → ready` / `ready → working` BETWEEN turns, (c) state never wedged, and (d) the final state is `ready`. The `requires` are `headless_mode` + `tool_calls`; the **Primitive exercised** is `tool_calls` + `headless_mode`, which map to `capabilities.json` keys `tool_calls: true` and `headless_mode: true` (both confirmed true).

**agent_supports: yes.** aider's REPL is natively multi-turn and runs tools inline. One process holds the session open and appends every prompt + reply to the same per-cwd `.aider.chat.history.md`; under `--yes-always` aider auto-accepts file-add and shell prompts so tool-call turns don't stall at a permission gate (unlike the modern claudecode default that forced the claudecode sibling to drop tool calls). `capabilities.json` records `tui: true`, `tool_calls: true`, `git_integration: true`; aider emits `> Applied edit to <file>` (file-edit tool) and `> Running <cmd>` (shell tool) blockquotes that the parser recognises (parser.go `appliedEditRE` / `runningRE`). The committed `drive-aider-interactive.sh` tmux driver chains many send+wait_turn cycles in one live REPL — so a multi-turn, tool-bearing session is directly drivable.

**daemon_capability: full.** The aider adapter watches `FilesUnderCWD{Filename: ".aider.chat.history.md", Parser: RawLineParser}` — markdown, not JSONL. Judging each spec signal against the events the daemon actually emits:

*Within-turn no-flicker (robustly full).* The parser opens a turn on each `#### ` user line and, within that turn, emits `assistant_message` at every `> Tokens:` line and `tool_result` (open+close coalesced) at every `> Applied edit`/`> Running` line — but deliberately NOT `turn_done`, because under `--yes-always` aider may re-prompt the model within one turn. None of these intermediate event kinds is terminal in the state classifier: only `turn_done` with no open tool and no trailing question flips `working → ready` (state_classifier.go). So the within-turn invariant — state stays continuously `working` across many interleaved assistant fragments and tool-call/result pairs — holds robustly, which is exactly the parser-coherence property this scenario stress-tests. `contains_tool_call: true` is fully observed via the `tool_result` events.

*Between-turn arc (full, with a recipe-cadence caveat).* End-of-turn is synthesized OUT-OF-BAND by `IdleFlush` after the transcript is quiet for `aiderIdleTurnDoneAfter` (1500ms), because aider's idle TUI prompt never lands in the markdown history. The daemon's `SessionDetector` polls working sessions every `staleWorkingRefreshInterval` (5s), so the effective `working → ready` latency after a turn's last line is ~5s. This is a real, in-Source trace handled correctly: the IdleFlush-synthesized `turn_done` IS the terminal event the classifier consumes, and the daemon emits a full per-turn `working → ready` arc whenever the next prompt does not outrun that window. The canonical recipe paces prompts with ≥12s sleeps (3× the idle window + ample slack over the 5s poll), so all four per-turn `ready → working → ready` arcs are emitted and observed. The daemon mis-handles nothing here — there is no spec-required observable it drops, and a trace exists for every assertion — so this is `full`, not `bug` and not `incapable`.

The known fragility is purely a recipe-cadence property, NOT a daemon defect: `TestParser_NewUserPromptAfterMultiCall_StaysWorking` pins that a `####` arriving while the turn is still open (next prompt lands before IdleFlush) opens the new turn WITHOUT an intervening `turn_done`, so the session stays `working` across that boundary and the per-turn reset is lost. Over N boundaries the cumulative blur risk compounds — but this is a property of how fast the DRIVER sends the next prompt, fully mitigated by the recipe's ≥12s inter-turn sleeps. It belongs in `caveats`, not in a `daemon_capability` downgrade (per #476: a narrow non-spec gap stays `full` + caveat).

**driver_capability: ready.** The recipe needs only `send`, `wait_turn`, and `sleep`, repeated four times — every step type is implemented in `drive-aider-interactive.sh` (send, slash, wait_turn, interrupt, sleep). `wait_turn` blocks on a new `> Tokens:` line; the sleeps cover the ~5s effective IdleFlush latency. No gapped primitive (restart/reset/resume/sigkill/keys/fork) is required by this scenario, so the driver has everything the recipe needs.

## Why full/ready (and where the old `partial` went)

Under the old single-axis schema this cell was `irrlicht_observes: partial`, with the partiality entirely attributable to the between-turn arc COUNT being fragile under fast prompting. Splitting per #476: the daemon emits every event the spec requires (within-turn coherence + per-turn arcs via IdleFlush + final `ready`), and the driver implements every step the recipe needs. The fragility is a recipe-cadence concern (next `####` outrunning the 1.5s idle window + 5s poll), mitigated by the canonical ≥12s sleeps and surfaced as a caveat — it is neither a daemon mis-handling (`bug`) nor an architectural blind spot (`incapable`: a trace exists and the 3-state model represents it fine), nor a missing driver step (`gap:*`: send + wait_turn + sleep are all present). So the honest split is `daemon_capability: full` + `driver_capability: ready`, with the compounding-blur risk preserved in caveats.

The final `ready` state is reached regardless (IdleFlush always fires once the session goes quiet at teardown), and `has_waiting_state: false` holds because plain prompts never trip the question/imperative waiting cue.

## Matrix drift

The coverage row already carries `daemon_capability: unknown`, `driver_capability: ready`, note "idle-flush windows compound on long sessions". This assessment resolves the `unknown` to `full` (driver already `ready`); the maintainer should update the coverage cell's `daemon_capability` to `full`.
caveats: Idle-flush windows compound on long sessions: each of the N turn boundaries independently needs the next #### prompt to NOT outrun the ~1.5s idle window + 5s detector poll. If any prompt lands before IdleFlush fires, that boundary blurs (TestParser_NewUserPromptAfterMultiCall_StaysWorking) and the session stays working across it — losing one of the expected per-turn resets. The canonical recipe mitigates this with ≥12s inter-turn sleeps; more turns = higher cumulative blur risk than basic-turn. This is a recipe-cadence property, not a daemon defect.

feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant: aider writes no in-band turn-end marker, so each between-turn working→ready flip is synthesized only by IdleFlush after ~1.5s quiet + the 5s detector poll (~5s effective latency). The per-turn arcs are observable only when every prompt is followed by ≥~5s idle (canonical recipe's ≥12s sleep + wait_turn settle).

The within-turn no-flicker invariant is robust: assistant_message (per `> Tokens:` line) and tool_result (per `> Applied edit`/`> Running` line) are non-terminal in the state classifier, so state stays continuously working across many interleaved fragments and tool calls within one turn. The only cadence-sensitive surface is the between-turn boundary.
cost_tracking: false — per-turn Contribution carries token counts (sent/received) from each `> Tokens:` line but provider cost is absent unless aider prints a `, $… message` suffix.

Exact assistant wording, per-turn token counts, tool-result content, and sub-second timing vary run-to-run (model-output variance); only the structural multi-turn arc, the within-turn no-flicker property, single-session count, and final ready state are stable across re-records.

sources.note: FilesUnderCWD{Filename: .aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser}; per-process Parser instance tracking idle-flush state; CommandPattern /aider — markdown transcript, not JSONL.

Turn opens on `#### `; `> Tokens:` emits assistant_message (NOT turn_done) carrying a per-call Tokens snapshot; `> Applied edit`/`> Running` emit tool_result (open+close coalesced under --yes-always); turn-end synthesized via IdleFlush after aiderIdleTurnDoneAfter (1500ms); comment documents ~5s effective working→ready latency.

TestParser_ToolCall_AppliedEdit / TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell (tool_result events); TestParser_MultiTurn_EmitsAssistantMessagePerTurn (assistant_message per turn, no in-band turn_done); TestParser_IdleFlush_AfterMultipleModelCalls_EmitsTurnDone (idle synthesizes turn_done — the trace that makes daemon_capability full); TestParser_NewUserPromptAfterMultiCall_StaysWorking (fast next prompt → no per-turn reset — the recipe-cadence compounding caveat, not a daemon defect).

Only turn_done (no open tool, no trailing question) flips working→ready; assistant_message and tool_result are non-terminal — this is what makes the within-turn no-flicker invariant robustly observable across many interleaved fragments and tool calls.

staleWorkingRefreshInterval = 5 * time.Second — the working-session poll cadence that sets the ~5s effective working→ready latency for idle-flush turn-end and underlies the between-turn arc cadence-sensitivity that the ≥12s recipe sleeps mitigate.

tool_calls: true, headless_mode: true (the two requires/Primitive-exercised keys for this scenario, both true), tui: true, git_integration: true, streaming_output: true; cost_tracking: false, structured_output_jsonl: false (markdown transcript).

tmux REPL driver implementing send / slash / wait_turn / interrupt / sleep; wait_turn blocks on a new `> Tokens:` line; keeps one aider session alive across all send/wait_turn cycles. The recipe needs only send + wait_turn + sleep (all present) — basis for driver_capability: ready and agent_supports: yes (native multi-turn REPL with inline tools under --yes-always).

Captured daemon events from a fast-prompted run: single ready→working, no intermediate working→ready flips before terminal process_exited — direct evidence of the idle-flush-compounding caveat at the between-turn boundary when prompts outrun the idle window (mitigated by the ≥12s recipe sleeps).

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a model out-of-band (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL plus the matching provider key/endpoint — e.g. a local LM Studio / OpenAI-compatible server). Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs ~5min on the OpenRouter OAuth prompt; the live REPL needs a model that actually answers each prompt and applies each edit. THIS CELL'S RECORDING IS DEFERRED: no provider key was present at authoring time, so the spec is committed as the documented target and the recording is captured once aider auth exists in-env.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it can send four prompts to one long-lived session
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

aider runs under --yes-always (capabilities.json permission_hooks:false), so every file-add / shell confirmation auto-answers and no tool-call turn stalls at a permission gate — without this, state would legitimately enter waiting and the no-waiting invariant would break
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in the transcript before sending the first prompt

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears

Each send asks aider to make one small file edit; under --yes-always the edit auto-applies and the parser emits a collapsed tool_result from the `> Applied edit to <file>` marker. wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears (signals aider finished one model call). aider has NO turn-end transcript marker — end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1.5s quiet, surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector poll (~5s effective latency)
After the fourth wait_turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session

script.text: Create a file notes.txt containing exactly the line: alpha
Append a line to notes.txt containing exactly: beta
Append a line to notes.txt containing exactly: gamma
Append a line to notes.txt containing exactly: delta

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession (proc-<PID>) event in ready within ~2s of REPL launch (aider's transcript is lazy, so the pre-session is the launch-time observable)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the live aider session_id

events.jsonl contains FOUR state_transition→working / →ready arcs on the SAME session_id, one per prompt — the per-turn working→ready flip is synthesized by IdleFlush and must be observed before the next prompt re-enters working (this is why the inter-turn sleeps are ≥12s, covering the ~5s effective latency with ample slack since fast models like gpt-5-nano otherwise outrun the idle window and coalesce turns; with shorter sleeps the next #### prompt opens a new turn with no intervening turn_done and the per-turn reset is lost — and the risk COMPOUNDS over four turns, the matrix's observes:partial caveat)

WITHIN each turn, state stays continuously working across the interleaved assistant fragments and the edit tool_result — NO state_transition to ready fires mid-turn (no flicker between the model fragments and the `> Applied edit` marker and the turn's end)

transcript.jsonl contains four user lines and at least four collapsed tool_result events (one `> Applied edit to notes.txt` per turn) — the contains_tool_call signal; the phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the tool call is asserted only via the working span it sustains
across the entire session, NO state_transition to waiting appears (aider runs under --yes-always, so every edit auto-applies and never gates)
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the session at REPL teardown (one long-lived session, not four exits)

per-turn token contribution (TotalTokens) accumulates monotonically from each `> Tokens:` snapshot — observable in the parser's assistant_message events but NOT expressible in the expected.jsonl phase DSL (no metric form); aider's cost_tracking is false so no provider dollar cost is carried

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Claude Code is designed precisely for long, multi-turn engineering sessions: many turns, each turn streaming several assistant text fragments and multiple tool calls (Read, Edit, Bash, Grep, WebFetch). The daily-driver workload of this very codebase is the canonical example, and irrlicht's claudecode adapter is the most-exercised adapter in production. The four Expected bullets are all within transport.

**Bullet 1 — within each turn: state stays `working` continuously.** This is the core anti-flicker invariant. The parser's terminal-stop_reason gate (parser.go:286-298) only flips state back to `ready` when an assistant message lands with `end_turn` / `stop_sequence` / `refusal` AND no further open tool_use. Intermediate streaming assistant messages, intermediate tool_use/tool_result pairs, and intra-turn user-role messages (tool_result wrappers) are all absorbed without state churn — `scanMessageContent` (parser.go:305-329) accumulates them onto the parsed event but does not emit a state_transition. Issue #102 history (referenced from parser.go around the interrupt-flag logic) and the live recording fixtures show this works at scale.

**Bullet 2 — between turns: `working → ready` at turn end, `ready → working` on next prompt.** Standard turn-arc behaviour; one `state_transition` pair per turn boundary.

**Bullet 3 — state never gets stuck in any single state across the entire session.** The phase contract for this scenario asserts a per-turn arc invariant across N turns. As long as each turn's transcript closes with a terminal stop_reason (which Claude Code does for healthy turns) and a fresh `user` message lands for the next turn, neither stickiness mode is reachable.

**Bullet 4 — after the final turn: state reaches `ready`.** Follows from bullet 2 applied to the final turn. The phase contract closes on a `ready` assertion.

## What irrlicht does NOT see (and why that's OK)

The spec asks about state-level continuity, not about counting tool calls or assistant fragments. irrlicht does record `ToolUses` and `ToolResultIDs` on each parsed event (parser.go:305-329), and the `PendingContributor` interface surfaces in-progress turn cost — both available as metrics if the recipe wants them. But the four Expected bullets are exclusively state-level and fully covered.

## Notes on stress sizing

The recording is necessarily a compressed proxy for a real long session: a handful of turns with two-to-five tool calls each is enough to exercise the no-flicker code paths. A truly long capture (hours, hundreds of turns) is impractical to record deterministically; the phase contract's per-turn invariants generalise.
caveats: The recording cannot reproduce a hours-long real engineering session deterministically; the recipe uses a compressed proxy (several turns × several tool calls). The no-flicker invariant generalises from the proxy because it's a property of every individual turn.

If a turn hits a permission prompt or AskUserQuestion mid-flight, state will legitimately enter `waiting` and the no-flicker assertion must allow `working ↔ waiting` (not just exclude `ready`). The recipe should either pre-approve tools or scope the invariant to exclude `waiting`.
If a tool result is dropped or the transcript truncates mid-turn, state could stick in `working` — issue #321-class regressions. The 'never stuck' invariant is the regression guard for that family.

sources.note: Section 'Feature: Long agentic session (stress)' (lines 149-156) — four Expected bullets covering per-turn and session-wide invariants.
Terminal stop_reason gate (286-298) and scanMessageContent (305-329) implement the no-flicker contract across intra-turn tool/text streaming.
PendingContributor + JSONL source — in-progress turn cost and tool-call counts available if the scenario wants supplementary metrics.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)

Claude Code launched in auto mode (Read/Bash/Write pre-approved) so no turn trips a permission gate — otherwise state would legitimately enter waiting and the no-flicker invariant would need to allow it
cwd under <staging>/cwd/ contains a CHANGELOG.md file (driver writes a stub if not present)
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/
Driver seeds CHANGELOG.md in cwd with a small fixture (e.g. '## 0.4.0 - 2026-05-01\n- initial') so turn 1's Read finds something
Auto mode is enabled by the driver (Shift+Tab until 'auto mode on' appears in the footer) so Bash/Read/Write are auto-classified

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: alpha
Reply with exactly the word: beta
Reply with exactly the word: gamma
Reply with exactly the word: delta

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready after launch
for EACH of the 4 turns: exactly one ready→working transition, at least one tool_use event inside the working span, exactly one working→ready transition at turn end
across the entire session, NO state_transition to ready fires inside any turn's working span (no flicker between failing/succeeding tool_use and the next tool_use inside the same turn)
across the entire session, NO state_transition to waiting fires (auto mode means no permission gate)
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains exactly 4 user lines and at least 8 assistant lines (tool_use + final reply per turn)

metadata.notes:

daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready
applicable: yes

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready
applicable: yes

## Reasoning

`long-agentic-session-stress` (scenario-meanings.md): the defining behavior is a long agentic turn carrying a large volume of interleaved assistant fragments and tool-call/result pairs, validating that state tracking stays coherent across the burst — within the turn state stays continuously `working` (no flicker to ready/waiting between tool calls), the turn settles cleanly to `ready`, and the session is never stuck. Primitive exercised: `tool_calls` + `headless_mode`, stress-testing the parser's ability to stay coherent across many sequential function_call/function_call_output pairs in ONE turn. Explicitly *Not to be confused with* `autonomous-loop` (no user prompts between iterations) or `self-correction-iteration` (a short retry cycle within one turn, not a high-volume burst).

**agent_supports: yes.** Codex CLI 0.130.0 drives shell work through its exec/`function_call` tool and runs many sequential commands autonomously inside a single turn with NO permission gate (capabilities.json permission_hooks:false), so the agent never stops to ask the user mid-burst. A prompt that asks it to explore a directory — pwd, then ls -la, then wc -l on several files, then summarize — deterministically produces a chain of many function_call/function_call_output pairs in one turn before the final task_complete. This is exactly the high-volume single-turn burst the scenario targets, and unlike claudecode (which refuses auto-mode for paid models and stalls tool-call turns at a permission gate, forcing the claudecode recipe into a 4-plain-turn proxy), codex can exercise the real many-tool-call shape natively. capabilities.json records tool_calls:true, headless_mode:true, tui:true.

**daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready.** The codex parser (parser.go) models each tool call as a paired `function_call` (open) / `function_call_output` (close) event, and a completed command always emits its function_call_output which CLOSES the open call (HasOpenToolCall clears), so the classifier never treats an intermediate tool result as a `waiting` gate or a `ready` settle — the whole burst stays a single contiguous `working` span. The turn settles to `ready` only at the canonical `task_complete` → `turn_done` signal (parser.go lines 155-170), whose explicit purpose is to suppress the assistant_message working→ready→working flicker that intermediate assistant fragments would otherwise cause at the start of every turn. Net observable: one ready→working at the prompt, ONE contiguous working span across the entire many-call burst, one working→ready at task_complete, final ready. The `partial` (matching the coverage matrix) reflects the documented dependence on the `task_complete` turn-done signal: if codex ever omits or delays task_complete (the fallback risk noted in the matrix re: #105 stale-tool timer flicker, since closed), the daemon would fall back to assistant_message heuristics and could mis-time the final settle — so the cell is observable but rests on one load-bearing signal rather than redundant evidence.

This cell is observationally DISTINCT from the neighbouring `codex × self-correction-iteration` (a short failing-then-corrected two-cycle retry asserting continuity across ONE errored result) and `codex × auto-executed-tool-call` (a single happy-path open/close): this cell asserts the parser's coherence across a HIGH VOLUME of sequential ungated tool calls in one turn — the stress dimension neither neighbour exercises.

## Conclusion

Applicable: yes. codex natively runs a long single-turn tool-call burst with no permission gate, and the daemon observes the full arc as one contiguous working span (each function_call_output closes its open call; task_complete is the only ready settle). Recipe: an interactive prompt that induces many sequential exec calls in one turn (pwd → ls -la → wc -l on the first few files → summarize), wait_turn (generous timeout for a longer turn), trailing sleep. Spec asserts user-observable state/topology only — session-birth ready, one ready→working, a contiguous working dwell across the burst with no flicker to ready/waiting mid-turn, one working→ready, final ready — never specific tool output text, command count, or token totals (all model/OS-dependent).
caveats: Assert user-observable state/topology only — do NOT assert specific exec tool output text, the exact number of commands codex chooses to run, or token totals; all are model/OS-dependent and not stable across re-records.

irrlicht_observes is partial (matching the coverage matrix): coherence of the burst rests on the single task_complete→turn_done settle signal; the no-mid-turn-flip invariant is the load-bearing assertion and is exactly what the codex open-call + turn_done logic guarantees.

Distinct from the committed codex × self-correction-iteration (short two-cycle retry) and codex × auto-executed-tool-call (single happy-path call): this cell stresses coherence across a HIGH VOLUME of sequential tool calls in one turn.

sources.note: long-agentic-session-stress: a realistic high-volume turn validates state tracking stays coherent — within a turn state stays working (no flicker), clean working→ready at turn boundaries, never stuck; primitive = tool_calls + headless_mode; distinct from autonomous-loop and self-correction-iteration
tool_calls:true, headless_mode:true, tui:true, permission_hooks:false — codex runs many sequential ungated tool calls autonomously inside one turn

function_call/function_call_output modeled as paired open/close; a completed command's function_call_output clears HasOpenToolCall (no waiting); task_complete→turn_done (lines 155-170) is the only ready settle, suppressing intermediate assistant_message flicker so the whole burst is one contiguous working span
coverage cell codex × long-agentic-session-stress = agent_supports:yes / irrlicht_observes:partial (#105 stale-tool timer flicker closed; task_complete dependence creates fallback risk)
neighbouring tool-call cell: short failing-then-corrected two-cycle retry asserting no-flicker across ONE errored result; this cell adds the high-volume stress dimension

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.codex/config.toml or OPENAI_API_KEY)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver launches `codex --no-alt-screen` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits for the TUI banner + MCP boot before sending the prompt

No auto-mode step is needed: codex has no permission gate (capabilities.json permission_hooks:false), so the shell tool runs every command autonomously inside codex's sandbox — this is exactly why codex can capture the REAL many-tool-call single-turn burst, whereas the claudecode recipe for this scenario must fall back to 4 plain prompts (modern claudecode refuses auto-mode for paid models and stalls tool-call turns at a permission gate)

The prompt is fully self-contained — it tells codex to CREATE its own files and then run many commands on them, so the per-run cwd starting empty doesn't shrink the burst (an early attempt that only said 'ls then wc the existing files' produced just 2 calls because the fresh cwd had nothing to wc)

wait_turn blocks until a new event_msg/task_complete line appears in the rollout, so the trailing sleep never races a still-running turn; the timeout is generous because this is a deliberately LONG turn

script.text:
Do all of the following in THIS single turn, using your shell tool, running each as a SEPARATE command one at a time (do not combine them, do not write a script, do not ask me anything): 1) run `pwd`; 2) create three files with `printf 'line1
line2
line3
' > a.txt`, then `printf 'x
y
' > b.txt`, then `seq 1 20 > c.txt` (three separate commands); 3) run `ls -la`; 4) run `wc -l a.txt`, then `wc -l b.txt`, then `wc -l c.txt` (three separate commands); 5) run `cat a.txt`; 6) run `head -3 c.txt`; then finally summarize in one short paragraph how many lines each file had. Run every numbered command individually so I can see each one execute.

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession_created (proc-<PID>) event in ready within 1000 ms of agent launch
events.jsonl contains a transcript_new for the rollout session with a distinct session_id, born ready
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the rollout session_id
events.jsonl contains exactly one state_transition→working on the rollout session_id when the prompt is sent (start of the agentic burst)
transcript.jsonl contains MANY function_call / function_call_output pairs in the single turn (at least 5: pwd, ls -la, and ≥3 wc -l calls) — the high-volume burst
across the entire turn, NO state_transition to ready fires between successive function_call/function_call_output pairs (one contiguous working span, no flicker mid-burst)
across the entire turn, NO state_transition to waiting fires (codex has no permission gate; every command auto-executes)
events.jsonl contains exactly one state_transition→ready on the rollout session_id once the final tool call resolves and task_complete fires (turn end)
final session state is ready (not stuck working)
events.jsonl contains a process_exited on the rollout session at REPL teardown

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: gap:live-session

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: gap:live-session

## Reasoning

This is a REASSESSMENT of a previously non-green cell (prior verdict: yes / bug / ready, recorded known_failing 3/4). Re-derived from scratch against opencode CLI v1.14.50 and the current daemon code. The prior `daemon_capability: bug` was a MISLABEL: the daemon does not mis-handle the terminal settle — it is a process-exit-before-poll RACE that is owned by the recording driver, not the daemon. The verdict flips to daemon_capability: full, and the real non-green axis is the driver.

long-agentic-session-stress asserts the daemon keeps a long, tool-heavy session coherently observed: state stays continuously `working` across MANY interleaved assistant-message / tool-call / tool-result iterations, no spurious flicker to ready/waiting mid-work, and a clean settle to ready when the turn ends. opencode supports tool calls (capabilities.json tool_calls:true) and in headless default (`opencode run`) auto-executes them inside its own sandbox with no permission gate, so the burst never enters waiting. Session state lives in opencode's SQLite store; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the UUID-keyed ses_… session.

Daemon path (full): the opencode watcher (watcher.go scanParts) reads new part rows and emits one EventActivity per scan, setting Terminal=true when it sees a step-finish part with a turn-ending reason (isTerminalPart: stop/interrupted/length/error/content-filter) or a message-level error (isErrorMessage, #493). A tool-calls step-finish reason is NOT terminal, so a long write→read→edit tool sequence inside one turn stays one contiguous `working` span — exactly the coherence this scenario stresses. The settle to ready happens in the application layer: session_detector_activity.go short-circuits the debounce on a Terminal activity and calls processActivity, which records the working→ready transition ("agent finished turn → ready"). metrics.go carries no state logic (pure token accounting). This settle path is PROVEN correct on the sibling headless recordings basic-turn (events.jsonl seq 701 working→ready lands 39ms BEFORE process_exited at seq 702) and self-correction-iteration (ready seq 4203 lands 12ms before process_exited seq 4204) — same driver, same source, clean settle. So given the terminal step-finish part while the process is alive, the daemon settles correctly: daemon_capability is full.

Driver gap (root cause of the prior known_failing): the existing recording's events.jsonl shows the long turn's terminal step-finish reason=stop landing in the WAL at seq 4761 (07.379s) — ~175ms AFTER process_exited at seq 4760 (07.204s). Because headless `opencode run` exits at turn end, the daemon's poller never reads the terminal part while the session's process is alive, so no terminal working→ready transition is recorded (no "agent finished turn → ready" line; the session is left in `working` at observation end, with only debounce_terminal at seq 4762). This is a timing race created by the DRIVER killing the process at turn end, not the daemon mis-parsing anything — and it is exactly how OPEN issue #492 frames it (labeled enhancement / scope:tooling, NOT bug): "the headless `opencode run` process exits at turn end, so the final step-finish reason=stop lands in the SQLite store ~140–175ms after process_exited, and the terminal working→ready settle is missed. A long-lived REPL process would fix that too."

The fix mechanism already exists in the driver: #495 added a LIVE-TUI path (run_live) to drive-opencode-interactive.sh that launches the opencode TUI under tmux and keeps ONE process alive across the whole step-script (send / wait_turn / reset_session / slash / sleep). A long-lived process never exits at turn end, so the daemon polls the terminal step-finish while the session is still alive and the working→ready settle lands normally — clearing the race. BUT the routing only engages run_live when the recipe carries a `reset_session` or `slash` step (drive-opencode-interactive.sh line ~355); this scenario's recipe is send/wait_turn/sleep only, so it still routes to the headless path and hits the race. Hence driver_capability: gap:live-session — the live/long-lived-process step path exists but this recipe is not wired to it. This is queued tooling work (route this scenario to run_live, or add an opt-in), NOT a daemon bug and NOT a freeze.

MODEL note: opencode's default is the local LM Studio model lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored (~/.config/opencode/opencode.json); the driver passes no --model. gemma 2B is slow and may be unreliable at sustaining a long multi-tool loop; if it will not sustain the burst that is a model/infra limit, not a daemon/opencode incapability (tool_calls:true).
caveats: Reconciled post-record (2026-05-27): routed onto the long-lived run_live path via the 'live' marker (commit f6919c45); cell recorded 4/4 green — driver_capability gap:live-session->ready.

The recordable target is now via the live-TUI driver path (run_live, added in #495), which keeps one opencode TUI process alive across the whole turn so the terminal step-finish is polled before exit. The current recipe (send/wait_turn/sleep) routes to the headless path; route it to run_live (the driver opts in on reset_session/slash steps today) to clear the race and re-record toward green.
opencode exposes no CLI-assignable session IDs; the driver discovers the ses_… session id by querying SQLite keyed on the per-run cwd.

The phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the stress is asserted via the single sustained working span + the no-waiting invariant; the high tool-call volume (≥5 function_call/output pairs) is verified in the SQLite store / transcript out-of-band at record time.

The local gemma 2B default model is slow and may be unreliable at sustaining a LONG multi-tool loop in one turn; if it will not sustain the burst the limitation is the model, not opencode/daemon (capabilities.json tool_calls:true).

sources.note: PROVES the miss is a process-exit race, not daemon mishandling: terminal step-finish lands as transcript_activity at seq 4761 (07.379s) ~175ms AFTER process_exited at seq 4760 (07.204s); no terminal state_transition→ready is recorded — the session is left in working with only debounce_terminal (seq 4762).

PROVES the daemon settle path is correct (daemon_capability: full): same headless driver, working→ready ("agent finished turn → ready") at seq 701 lands 39ms BEFORE process_exited at seq 702 — when the terminal step-finish is polled while the process is alive, the daemon settles cleanly. self-correction-iteration shows the same (seq 4203 ready 12ms before seq 4204 process_exited).

scanParts/isTerminalPart: a step-finish with reason stop/interrupted/length/error/content-filter sets Terminal=true on EventActivity; a tool-calls reason is non-terminal so a long multi-tool turn stays ONE working span. metrics.go carries no state logic — settle is entirely activity-driven.

On a Terminal activity the detector short-circuits the debounce and calls processActivity, which records the working→ready settle. Process exit (KindProcessExited, pid_manager.go) emits no state transition, so a terminal part arriving AFTER process_exited produces no settle — the driver-owned race.

OPEN, labeled enhancement / scope:tooling / scope:opencode — frames this cell's known_failing explicitly as a DRIVER problem ('headless `opencode run` process exits at turn end … the terminal working→ready settle is missed. A long-lived REPL process would fix that too.'). Not a daemon bug.

run_live (added in #495) launches the opencode TUI under tmux and keeps ONE process alive across send/wait_turn/reset_session/slash/sleep — the long-lived path that clears the #492 race. Routing (line ~355) engages it only on reset_session/slash steps; this recipe (send/wait_turn/sleep) still routes headless, hence driver_capability: gap:live-session.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its configured default model (~/.config/opencode/opencode.json; the local LM Studio model lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored at authoring time). The driver passes NO --model, so it uses that default
opencode's SQLite store exists at ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db (created on first run); the driver queries it for the ses_… session id and exports the parts transcript
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: RUN_LIVE — the leading {"type":"live"} marker forces drive-opencode-interactive.sh onto its run_live path (router engages on any of reset_session/slash/interrupt/keys/restart/sigkill/mid_turn_send/live). run_live launches ONE opencode TUI under tmux in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and keeps that SAME process alive across ALL the sequential send/wait_turn turns; opencode auto-assigns the ses_… session id (no CLI-assignable id) and the driver discovers it from the SQLite session table keyed on that cwd

WHY run_live (not headless `opencode run`): headless spawns a FRESH subprocess per send that EXITS ~140–175ms before the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore poller reads its terminal `step-finish reason=stop`, so the final working→ready settle lands in the store AFTER process_exited and is missed (issue #492 — a DRIVER process-exit-before-poll race, NOT a daemon bug). A long-lived run_live process never exits at turn end, so the poller reads the terminal step-finish while the session is alive and the FINAL settle lands normally — this is the deliberate fix that lets the cell drop its prior known_failing

WHY a genuinely LONG multi-turn arc: the scenario stresses the daemon keeping ONE session coherently observed across MANY interleaved assistant-message / tool-call / tool-result iterations. Each of the six sequential turns gives opencode a small, fully-enumerated file task (create / write / read-back / list / append / summarize) so its agent loop chains real write/read/list tool calls, and the turns accumulate work across the run — a high tool-call volume and monotonic token accumulation across the whole session
WHY tool calls auto-execute without a gate: opencode runs its file/list tools inside its own sandbox with NO interactive permission prompt, so no turn enters waiting

WHY each turn is send → wait_turn → sleep: wait_turn blocks on run_live until the terminal step-finish total exceeds the pre-send baseline (the turn actually ended), and the inter-turn sleeps (5s) let each working→ready settle land before the next send re-enters working. The local gemma 2B default model is slow (~30–45s per turn) so the timeout is generous (900s). NOTE: the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore watcher observes the store via WAL-write-triggered scans with a 500ms minScanGap debounce, so back-to-back same-ses_ turns may COALESCE into one observed working→ready span — the spec asserts the SESSION-LEVEL arc + FINAL settle, not N separable per-turn arcs

script.text: Using your file tools, do all of this in THIS turn, one step at a time (do not ask me anything): create six files named step1.txt, step2.txt, step3.txt, step4.txt, step5.txt, step6.txt, and into each write exactly its step number on a single line (step1.txt contains 1, …, step6.txt contains 6). Then reply with exactly: created.
Using your file tools, read all six files step1.txt through step6.txt back and tell me the number each one contains. Then reply with exactly: read-back-done.

Using your file tools, list every file in the current directory, then create a file summary.md that lists one per line each stepN.txt filename followed by the number it contains. Then reply with exactly: summary-written.
Using your file tools, append a new line 'total=21' to summary.md, then read summary.md back and show me its full contents. Then reply with exactly: total-appended.
Using your file tools, create three more files notes1.txt, notes2.txt, notes3.txt, each containing a single line with its own name, then read all three back. Then reply with exactly: notes-done.
Using your file tools, list every file in the current directory one final time and tell me the total count of files you see. Then reply with exactly: all-done.

verify: events.jsonl shows the ses_… session appear in ready within ~3s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> presession may also appear; the arc and pid_discovered land on the UUID-keyed ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the ses_… session when the first multi-step prompt is sent (start of the long agentic burst)
across the entire run, NO state_transition to waiting fires (opencode auto-executes its file/list tools under run_live; no permission gate)

transcript.jsonl / the SQLite part table contains a HIGH VOLUME of tool parts across the six turns (≥5 function_call / function_call_output pairs: the file writes, read-backs, list calls, and summary writes) — verified out-of-band since the phase DSL has no tool-event form

token accumulation is monotonic across the turns (cum_input_tokens > 0, cum_output_tokens > 0, non-decreasing across the turn boundaries in events.jsonl / the replay .golden summary) — verified out-of-band since the phase DSL has no TotalTokens/delta form

after the FINAL turn the SAME ses_… session settles working→ready (the previously-truncated terminal settle is now captured under run_live; clean settle, not stuck working) — back-to-back turns may COALESCE under the WAL-poll debounce so per-turn arc separability is NOT asserted
final session state is ready

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

long-agentic-session-stress is a long multi-turn agentic session with several tool calls, stress-testing that irrlicht's transcript parser stays coherent across a large volume of interleaved assistant messages and tool-call/result pairs — no flicker WITHIN a turn, clean working→ready / ready→working BETWEEN turns, state never wedged. Pi supports this natively: an interactive pi REPL holds one rollout session open under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/<ts>_<UUID>.jsonl (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy) while accepting many prompts, several of which call tools (read/ls/grep against files staged in setup). Pi auto-executes read-only tools without a permission gate (unlike modern claudecode defaults), so tool-call turns don't stall — the recipe can therefore keep the tool calls that the claudecode sibling had to drop. Each assistant turn's stopReason=stop maps to turn_done, so the daemon observes one clean working→ready settle per turn on the SAME rollout session_id, with state resetting to ready between prompts and never entering waiting.

The cell is recorded with the INTERACTIVE pi driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh): 4-6 back-to-back send+wait_turn cycles in one tmux REPL, several eliciting tool calls, then a single teardown. Headless `pi --print` is single-shot (one reply → process_exited) so it cannot build a long multi-turn rollout; the interactive driver keeps the rollout alive for all turns and kills the tmux session once → a SINGLE process_exited.

irrlicht observes all of the lifecycle scaffolding: a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready, the UUID-keyed rollout's transcript_new, N clean working→ready arcs (one per prompt) on the rollout session, pid_discovered, and one process_exited at teardown.

## Why PARTIAL

The observes verdict is `partial` (not `yes`) because of the compaction caveat: if pi fires context compaction during the long rollout, the compaction summary interleaves as assistant content in the transcript. A compaction burst risks an extra working→ready→working flicker that is NOT a user turn boundary. Compaction does not fire deterministically on a compressed-proxy run of 4-6 turns, so the spec does NOT assert a compaction phase. The spec asserts that the FIRST N user-turn arcs reset cleanly and never enter waiting, and that there is a single process_exited — not an exact arc count — so an incidental compaction-driven extra arc does not fail the spec.
caveats: Pi cannot accept a pre-assigned UUID — it generates one itself. The interactive driver discovers the UUID from the session header on line 1 of the freshly-created rollout transcript.

COMPACTION INTERLEAVE: if pi fires context compaction during the long rollout, the compaction summary interleaves as assistant content and may produce an extra working→ready→working flicker that is not a user turn boundary. Compaction is not deterministic on a 4-6-turn proxy run, so the spec does NOT assert a compaction phase; it asserts the first N user-turn arcs reset cleanly with no waiting, not an exact arc count.

Exact assistant wording, per-turn token counts, tool-result content, and sub-second timing vary run-to-run (model-output variance). Only the structural multi-turn arc (N clean working→ready arcs on one rollout session), the single process_exited, and the final ready state are stable across re-records.
3s sleeps between wait_turn and the next send sit past the classifier's debounce window so each turn's working→ready flip is observed cleanly before the next prompt re-enters working.

sources.note: Identity.Name=pi, Source variant FilesUnderRoot under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/, ExactName{Name: "pi"} process matcher.
stopReason=stop on each assistant turn maps to turn_done (working→ready); tool_use/tool_result events stay within the working span; many turns on one session yield many clean arcs.
Interactive tmux REPL driver supporting send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep; keeps one pi session alive across all turns, then kills the tmux session once → single process_exited.
long-agentic-session-stress: per-turn ready→working→ready arcs with no within-turn flicker, no stuck state, over a large transcript volume with tool calls.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured model out-of-band)
tmux available — the interactive driver runs pi in a tmux REPL so it stays alive across all four turns
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

PARTIAL caveat (mirrored from coverage): if pi fires context compaction during the rollout, the compaction summary interleaves as assistant content and may add an extra working→ready→working flicker that is NOT a user turn boundary — compaction is not deterministic on a 4-turn proxy run, so the spec asserts the first four clean arcs and no waiting, not an exact arc count
setup: The interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ inside a tmux session, waits for the REPL 'Ready' line, then sends each prompt; it keeps ONE rollout alive across all four turns and kills the tmux session exactly once at the end → a single process_exited

pi writes one JSONL rollout file per session under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/ on launch (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy); the driver resolves that file and extracts the UUID from line 1 (pi assigns its own UUID; it can't be pre-set)

WHY tool calls are KEPT here (unlike the claudecode sibling, which had to drop them): pi auto-executes its bash tool with NO permission gate, so the tool-call turns never stall in waiting. The four prompts each elicit a real bash tool call against a file the session builds for itself (create → list → grep → read back), exercising interleaved assistant+tool_use+tool_result content across a long rollout — the actual stress this scenario names

WHY interactive (not headless `pi --print`): headless pi is single-shot and exits after one reply, so it can't build a long multi-turn rollout; keeping pi alive in the REPL across four send+wait_turn cycles lets the daemon observe four clean working→ready settles on one session

WHY the 3s sleeps between wait_turn and the next send: they sit past the classifier's debounce window so each turn's working→ready flip is observed cleanly before the next prompt re-enters working; without them the next send can land before the prior turn settles and the arcs blur. The trailing 5s sleep lets the final settle land before the single tmux teardown

script.text:
Using your bash tool, run: printf 'apple
banana
cherry
' > fruits.txt — then reply with exactly: created
Using your bash tool, run: ls -la — then reply with exactly: listed
Using your bash tool, run: grep banana fruits.txt — then reply with exactly: matched
Using your bash tool, run: cat fruits.txt — then reply with exactly: done

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new for the rollout session born ready within ~2s of REPL launch (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear; every turn arc, pid_discovered and teardown land on the UUID-keyed rollout session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the rollout session_id
for EACH of the four turns: exactly one state_transition→working / →ready arc on the rollout session_id, sustained across the auto-executed bash call (four clean arcs in order on one session)

transcript.jsonl contains four user lines and, across the rollout, four toolCall / toolResult pairs (the auto-executed bash calls: printf create, ls, grep, cat) — pi has no permission hook so each runs ungated
across the entire rollout, NO state_transition to waiting fires for the rollout session at any point (every tool call auto-executes; pi never gates)
the rollout session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session at the single tmux teardown (not four — the REPL is long-lived)

ACCEPT-EITHER: if pi fires compaction mid-rollout an extra working→ready→working arc may appear; the recording still passes because the spec asserts the first four clean arcs and no waiting, not an exact arc count
